title: Common Errors — The Bug Report
subtitle: The fossilised mistakes examiners see every day — and how to eradicate them
intro: This is a cross-cutting reference, not a module lesson. Use it before you submit any essay — it catches the errors that even careful Band 7 writers keep repeating. Each entry gives you the error pattern, why it is wrong, and the correction. Internalise these twenty fixes and you will often pick up an extra 0.5 band on Grammatical Accuracy alone.

rules.heading: Article and countability errors
rules.body: 'Most important problem is' → 'The most important problem is'. 'Many informations' → 'Much information'. 'In twenty-first century' → 'In the twenty-first century'.

rules.heading: Subject–verb agreement drift
rules.body: Long subjects with interrupters — find the head noun before writing the verb. 'The impact of rising temperatures on coastal communities (is / are) now impossible to ignore' → subject is 'impact' → 'is'.

rules.heading: Tense shift inside a paragraph
rules.body: Pick an anchor tense and justify every departure from it. 'The chart shows that production rose and then increases' → stay in past: 'rose and then plateaued'.

rules.heading: Preposition traps
rules.body: Discuss (no 'about'); depend ON; consist OF; interested IN; different FROM; effect ON; reason FOR; solution TO; increase IN.

rules.heading: Register blunders
rules.body: 'According to me' / 'In my point of view' → 'In my view' / 'I would argue that…'. 'Nowadays' overused → 'In recent decades', 'At present'. Contractions (don’t, can’t) → full forms in Task 2.

examples.sentence: Check every 'a / an / the / —' in your introduction and conclusion sentences — these paragraphs are where article errors cost the most marks.
examples.note: Make the article pass your first manual pass.

examples.sentence: Read each paragraph aloud in a single tense to catch drift. If a verb feels wrong when isolated, it usually is.
Scan for 'nowadays', 'very', 'really', 'a lot of', 'so', 'thing', 'people' — every one is a candidate for a precision upgrade.
Count your Band 8 markers: is there at least one participle clause, one inversion or cleft, one piece of hedging, and one nominalised subject?
Confirm that every 'this' and 'these' has an unambiguous referent in the previous sentence.

band6.text: The governments should to discuss about this problem because it effect the childrens future.
band6.issue: 'should to', 'discuss about', 'effect' as verb, 'childrens' plural+possessive muddle — 4 errors in 14 words.

band8.text: Governments should discuss this problem openly, as it directly affects children's futures.
band8.why: Bare infinitive after 'should', 'discuss' with no preposition, verb 'affect' (not 'effect'), and clean possessive 'children's'.

band8_marker: Band 8 writers treat proofreading as a distinct skill. They do not just re-read; they run specific audit passes: article pass, subject–verb pass, preposition pass, tense pass, repetition pass. Each pass catches a class of error. This discipline is what separates a rushed Band 6.5 essay from a polished Band 8.

common_errors.fix: Cliche + over-generalisation → 'Contemporary societies face a range of pressing challenges' or start with a more specific claim.

common_errors.error: 'There are many people who think that…'
common_errors.fix: Wordy cliche → 'Many argue that…' or 'It is widely claimed that…'

common_errors.error: 'In conclusion, I want to say that…'
common_errors.fix: → 'In conclusion, the evidence suggests…' / 'To conclude, the case for X rests on…'
Cliche in formal writing → 'Finally' / 'A further consideration is…'

common_errors.error: 'People have different opinions on this matter'
common_errors.fix: Vacuous filler → delete, replace with a specific claim.

common_errors.error: 'The environment is being destroyed day by day.'
common_errors.fix: → 'Environmental degradation has accelerated over recent decades.'
'Research' is uncountable → 'Research has shown that…' or 'Studies have shown…'

practice.title: Audit the essay fragment
practice.instruction: Click the single error in each sentence.

items.prompt: The impact of rising temperatures [A:on] coastal communities [B:are] [C:impossible] [D:to ignore].
The chart [A:shows] that production [B:rose] sharply and then [C:increases] [D:again].
In [A:my point of view], [B:governments] [C:should] invest [D:in] education.
items.explanation: 'In my point of view' is non-standard → 'In my view'.
'Research' is uncountable → 'Research has shown' or 'Studies have shown'.

items.prompt: The new policy [A:depends from] [B:the cooperation] [C:of several] [D:ministries].

items.explanation: 'Last but not least' is a cliche in academic writing → 'Finally' / 'A further consideration is…'

practice.title: Upgrade the cliche
practice.instruction: Replace the underlined phrase with a stronger alternative.

items.prompt: 'Nowadays, technology is changing our lives.' → Replace 'Nowadays'.
items.answer: In recent years / Over the past decade / At present

items.prompt: 'There are many people who think that…' → Replace the whole phrase.
items.answer: Many argue that… / It is widely believed that…
In my view / I would argue that / It is my contention that

items.prompt: 'It is a well-known fact that…' → Replace.
items.answer: There is broad consensus that… / Most analysts agree that…
a range of challenges / numerous obstacles / a host of difficulties
contemporary youth / today’s young adults / the younger generation
degrade the environment / inflict environmental damage / accelerate ecological decline

practice.title: Final sanity checks
practice.instruction: Pick the version an examiner would reward.

items.prompt: Which opening is strongest for a Task 2 essay?
options: 'Nowadays, there are many problems in the world.'
'The twenty-first century has ushered in a set of interlocking global challenges.'
'In today’s modern world, everything is changing.'
'People have different opinions about many things.'
'Kids today don’t read books anymore.'
'Children today rarely engage with traditional print media.'
'Nowadays, children don’t read book like before.'
'Kids don’t really read anymore.'
'In conclusion, I want to say that this issue is very important.'
'In conclusion, while the problem is complex, evidence favours a targeted intervention.'
'To sum up, many people think different things.'
'Last but not least, government should do something.'
'Everyone knows that pollution is bad.'
'There is broad scientific consensus that air pollution harms respiratory health.'
'It is 100% true that pollution kills people.'
'Many people say pollution is a problem.'
'The policies that was introduced in 2019 have succeeded.'
'The policies introduced in 2019 have succeeded.'
'The policies which introduced in 2019 have succeeded.'
'The policies was introduced in 2019 and have succeeded.'
'Obviously, this proves that automation destroys jobs.'
'Some evidence suggests that automation may displace certain categories of jobs.'
'It’s a fact that AI will take all jobs.'
'Nobody denies that automation is terrible.'
'Pollution is bad. Pollution kills people. Pollution is everywhere.'
'Air pollution has become a defining public-health concern; its effects are now documented across every major metropolitan area.'
'Pollution is very bad, and people die, and it is everywhere.'
'In conclusion, pollution is a problem.'

items.prompt: Pick the sentence using correct articles and tense.
options: 'Education is a key to prosperous society.'
'The education is key to a prosperous society.'
'Education is key to a prosperous society.'
'Education are key to the prosperous society.'
